name: How to Start Your First Homestead Garden
description: A step-by-step guide to growing your first real food garden, even with limited space and no prior experience.

step.name: Assess Your Space
step.text: Look at what you actually have — a backyard, a balcony, a sunny windowsill, or a community plot. Measure it. Take a photo. The four crops that pay off fastest are tomatoes, lettuce, herbs, and peppers. Even a 4x4 foot space can feed a family all summer.

step.name: Build Your Soil
step.text: Good soil grows good food. Start with a mix of potting soil and compost. If you're planting in the ground, loosen the top 6 inches and mix in compost. The goal is dark, crumbly soil that smells like earth, not chemicals. This is the foundation everything else builds on.

step.name: Plant the Four Fast Crops
step.text: Tomatoes, lettuce, herbs (basil/cilantro), and peppers. These four give you the fastest return on effort and space. Follow seed packet spacing — don't crowd them. Water immediately after planting. Label each spot so you remember what you planted where.

step.name: Water and Watch
step.text: Water deeply every 2-3 days, not a light sprinkle daily. Stick your finger in the soil — if it's dry an inch down, water. Watch for pests, but don't panic. Most 'problems' solve themselves if you wait a week. The garden teaches patience.

step.name: Harvest and Preserve
step.text: Pick tomatoes when they're fully colored, lettuce when leaves are palm-sized, herbs before they flower. Eat fresh first, then learn to preserve — canning, dehydrating, or freezing. One good harvest teaches more than a hundred articles. The RootedReadyLife Food Preservation Masterclass walks you through every method safely.
